---
title: "Day 2 (Tuesday): Trees and Modern Pipelines"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, ../assets/css/slides-theme.scss]
    slide-number: true
    incremental: true
    scrollable: true
    slide-level: 2
    html-math-method: katex
---

## Learning objectives

- Frame **Breiman’s two cultures**: same goals and problems, different **modeling approaches** (not “stats vs ML”)
- Build intuition for **decision trees** (`rpart`): recursive splits, partitions, and interpretable rules
- Walk through **gene × disease** (classification) and **gene × trait** (regression) trees step by step
- Read **`|>`** chains for tidy tables and `tidymodels` preprocessing
- Use **`tidymodels`** on **Palmer Penguins**: recipes, workflows, resampling, and tuning with honest holdout thinking

## Navigation

- [Teaching Hub](../index.qmd)
- [Curriculum Outline](../curriculum-outline.qmd)
- [Module 03 — Trees & cultures](../modules/module-03-trees-and-cultures.qmd)
- [Module 04 — Pipeline](../modules/module-04-pipeline.qmd)
- [Back: Monday deck](day-01-monday.qmd)
- [Next: Day 4 deck](day-04-thursday.qmd)

## Two cultures (Breiman, 2001)

Leo Breiman’s [*Statistical Modeling: The Two Cultures*](https://projecteuclid.org/journals/statistical-science/volume-16/issue-3/Statistical-Modeling--The-Two-Cultures/10.1214/ss/1009213726) argues the useful split is **not** “statistics vs machine learning.”

- Scientists often share the **same goals** (learn patterns, predict, support decisions) and work on the **same kinds of data**
- What differs is **how you build a model** — what you assume up front and what you optimize

## Data modeling culture

- Start with a **stochastic model** you can write down (Monday: `lm`, `glm`, lasso on known structure)
- Emphasize **parameters**, uncertainty, and whether the **model class** is adequate
- Strength: transparent assumptions; risk: the model may be wrong in shape even when it fits “well”

## Algorithmic modeling culture

- Treat the data-generating mechanism as **largely unknown**
- Let a **flexible algorithm** find structure; judge it by **predictive performance** on data not used to fit (today: trees; soon: cross-validation on penguins)
- Strength: captures interactions and nonlinear boundaries; risk: needs **careful evaluation** so flexibility does not fool you on the training rows

## Why trees today?

- **Nonlinear** boundaries and **interactions** without hand-written product terms (contrast Monday’s additive `glm`)
- **Readable rules:** each path is an if–then story on measurements
- Bridge from parametric models to **ensembles** (Thursday: forests, boosting)

## `rpart` in one slide

- **Classification:** `rpart(factor_y ~ ., data = ..., method = "class")` — splits improve class purity (Gini)
- **Regression:** `rpart(trait ~ GeneA + GeneB, data = ...)` — numeric $y$ uses **`method = "anova"`** (within-node variance); each leaf predicts a **mean** trait
- **`cp`:** complexity penalty — larger `cp` → simpler tree (see trait block after disease)
- **`control`:** `maxdepth`, `cp`, `minsplit` — same levers you will tune inside `tidymodels` later

## Gene × disease (same simulation as Monday)

- Binary **disease** from simulated **gene expression** — instructors know causal genes (**Gene1**, **Gene3**, **Gene4**, interaction)
- **Today:** classification tree on **all genes**, visualized in the **Gene1 × Gene3** plane (other genes at means)
- **Next:** classification tree grows **one split level at a time** (tree + partition side by side)
- Notebook: [logistic-regression-gene-disease.Rmd](../notebooks/logistic-regression-gene-disease.Rmd)

{{< include ../_includes/day02-gene-rpart-steps.qmd >}}

## Gene × trait (Monday simulation, regression tree)

- Continuous **trait** from **GeneA** / **GeneB** — same ground truth as Monday’s `lm` / lasso block
- **`rpart(trait ~ GeneA + GeneB, cp = ...)`** — sweep **`cp`** from `$cptable` to show how the predicted **surface** and tree change
- Notebook: [linear-regression-lasso.Rmd](../notebooks/linear-regression-lasso.Rmd)

{{< include ../_includes/day02-gene-rpart-trait.qmd >}}

## From trees to pipelines (Palmer Penguins)

- Monday compared models **on all rows** (in-sample); choosing lasso’s $\lambda$ used **leave-one-out CV** on the same $n$
- **Today (second half):** **hold out** some penguins for an honest test score; bundle preprocessing + model so resampling cannot leak
- Real data: [Palmer Penguins](../dataset-penguins.qmd) — Adelie vs Gentoo; we use **bill + island + sex + year** (not flipper/mass) so **hyperparameter tuning** shows a real tradeoff on the slides

{{< include ../_includes/day02-tidyverse-intro.qmd >}}

## Pipeline mindset (three ideas)

- **Split first** when any step learns from data (means, dummy levels, scaling)
- **One workflow object** runs preprocessing and the model together on each resample fold
- **Pick metrics before** you chase the best tuning knobs

## How the pieces fit together

| Package | Job in plain language |
|---------|------------------------|
| **`rsample`** | Decide which rows are for **fitting** vs **scoring** (holdout split, CV folds) |
| **`recipes`** | Describe **preprocessing** as a reusable checklist (dummy code factors, scale numbers, …) |
| **`parsnip`** | Name the **model family** and which knobs are still unknown — **no fit yet** |
| **`workflows`** | Glue recipe + model into **one** object so resampling never “forgets” preprocessing |
| **`tune`** | Try many knob settings; each try is scored only on **held-out** fold rows |
| **`yardstick`** | Turn predictions into numbers (ROC AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, …) with one grammar |

The walk-through below uses the same order: **data → recipe → spec → workflow → tune → fit → figures**.

## `rsample` — who gets to teach the model?

- **`initial_split(data, prop = 0.8, strata = outcome)`** — randomly hold out ~20% of rows as a **test** set you touch **once** at the end; `strata = outcome` keeps Adelie/Gentoo balance in both parts
- **`training(split)`** / **`testing(split)`** — extract the two tables from that split object
- **`vfold_cv(training(split), v = 5, strata = outcome)`** — chop the **training** rows into 5 folds for **cross-validation** while tuning; each fold is “train on 4 chunks, score on the 5th” so you never tune on the final test rows

**Why it matters:** scaling bill length to mean 0 uses **only** the rows in the current training fold — not future test penguins.

## `recipes` — preprocessing as a checklist

- **`recipe(y ~ ., data = train)`** — declare the prediction task: outcome `y`, every other column is a predictor (like a formula in `lm`, but for **steps** not coefficients)
- **`step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors())`** — turn **island** and **sex** into numeric 0/1 columns so `rpart` can split on them
- **`step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())`** — subtract mean and divide by SD **per numeric column** so mm, g, and year live on a comparable scale
- **`step_zv(all_predictors())`** — drop columns with **zero variance** (e.g. a dummy level that never appears in a small fold)
- Read the chain **top to bottom** with `|>`: data → step → step

**Two verbs students will see in docs:** `prep(recipe, training_data)` **learns** the rules (means, levels, scaling) from training rows only; `bake(recipe, new_data)` **applies** those same rules to validation or test rows. In a **workflow**, tidymodels calls these inside each CV fold for you.

## `parsnip` — model blueprint (not the fitted tree)

- **`decision_tree(tree_depth = tune(), min_n = tune())`** — “we want a classification tree; **depth** and **minimum leaf size** are still unknown” (`tune()` marks a knob for later)
- **`set_engine("rpart")`** — which **implementation** fits the tree (same idea as choosing `glm` vs another optimizer)
- **`set_mode("classification")`** — match a **factor** outcome (Adelie vs Gentoo), not a numeric one

**Nothing is fitted yet** — `parsnip` only stores the **family** and hyperparameters. Fitting happens after you plug in tuned values and call `fit()`.

## `workflows` — one object for the whole pipeline

- **`workflow() |> add_recipe(rec) |> add_model(mod_spec)`** — bundle preprocessing + model so every resample refit runs **both** in the right order
- **Why one object?** If you scaled bill length using **all** penguins before CV, test-fold information leaked into training — the workflow forces “prep on analysis rows, predict on assessment rows” automatically

## `tune` + `yardstick` — search knobs, score honestly

- **`tune()`** in the spec — marks which arguments `tune_grid()` should vary (here: `tree_depth`, `min_n`)
- **`tune_grid(workflow, resamples = folds, grid = ..., metrics = metrics)`** — for each knob combination, fit the **whole workflow** on each CV analysis set and score on the held-out assessment fold
- **`metric_set(roc_auc, accuracy, sens, spec)`** — commit to **these** scores before peeking at test data; `yardstick` computes them from predicted probabilities and class labels the same way every time
- **`select_best(tune_res, metric = "roc_auc")`** — pick the winning knob values from CV only
- **`finalize_workflow(wf, best)`** — insert those values into the unfitted workflow; **`fit(..., data = train)`** (or `last_fit` with a split) actually trains the final model

## Shared pipeline walk-through (penguins)

The next slides run the **canonical Adelie vs Gentoo** pipeline (same code as [Module 04](../modules/module-04-pipeline.qmd#canonical-pipeline-tuesday)): recipe → tuned `rpart` tree → diagnostic figures.

{{< include ../_includes/tidymodels-pipeline.qmd >}}

## Comparing many models (`workflowsets`, `stacks`)

- **`workflowsets`** — several model recipes on the **same** CV folds
- **`stacks`** — combine tuned models with a simple meta-learner (capstone after forests)

## Transition to Day 4

- Tuesday: pipeline **backbone** on penguins
- Day 4: **forests, boosting, neural nets**, PCA/impute/upsample in recipes, VIP and metrics

## End-of-day checklist

- Can you name Breiman’s **two cultures** in one sentence each?
- For the gene **classification** tree: what does **one split** do to the Gene1–Gene3 partition?
- For the gene **trait** tree: what does a larger **`cp`** do to the number of regions?
- What is the difference between **training**, **CV**, and **test** rows in the penguin workflow?
